items.title: How superior vena cava syndrome presents and is diagnosed
items.content:
SVC obstruction reduces venous return from the head, neck, and arms.
Malignancy causes most cases — lung cancer (especially small-cell and squamous) accounts for ~85% of malignant cases, and lymphoma is a leading cause in young adults — but intravascular devices now cause at least 40% of cases (benign).
Patients present with neck and facial swelling (especially around the eyes), dyspnea, and cough, with dilated neck and chest-wall collateral veins; symptoms worsen when bending forward or supine.
The diagnosis is clinical; the most significant chest-radiograph finding is superior mediastinal widening (usually right-sided), and CT best shows mediastinal anatomy with diminished central venous opacification and collaterals.
keyPoints: Lung cancer ~85% of malignant SVCS; lymphoma leads in young adults; devices cause ≥40% (benign)
Facial/neck swelling, dyspnea, cough, dilated chest-wall collateral veins; worse supine
Clinical diagnosis; CXR shows superior mediastinal widening, CT confirms
items.reference: SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME: "Lung cancer, particularly of small-cell and squamous cell histologies, accounts for ~85% of all cases of malignant origin."

items.title: Why tracheal obstruction is the truly life-threatening feature of SVCS
items.content:
Most SVCS is not immediately lethal, but airway compromise is.
The one potentially life-threatening complication of a superior mediastinal mass is tracheal obstruction, and upper airway obstruction demands emergent therapy; cerebral or laryngeal edema also signals a poorer prognosis and urgent evaluation.
Temporary relief comes from diuretics with a low-salt diet, head elevation, and oxygen; glucocorticoids have a limited role except with mediastinal lymphoma.
Definitive therapy depends on histology — radiation is primary for non-small-cell lung cancer and other solid tumors, while chemotherapy is effective for small-cell lung cancer, lymphoma, or germ cell tumors; endovascular self-expanding stents give rapid relief and are increasingly used first.
keyPoints: Tracheal obstruction (and cerebral/laryngeal edema) is the emergency in SVCS
Temporizing: diuretics/low salt, head elevation, oxygen; steroids mainly for lymphoma
Radiation for NSCLC/solid tumors; chemotherapy for SCLC/lymphoma/germ cell; stents for rapid relief
items.reference: TREATMENT (SVCS): "The one potentially life-threatening complication of a superior mediastinal mass is tracheal obstruction. Upper airway obstruction demands emergent therapy."

items.title: How malignant pericardial tamponade is diagnosed and drained
items.content:
Pericardial disease is common at autopsy (5–10% of cancer patients) but tamponade demands prompt drainage.
Common symptoms are dyspnea, cough, chest pain, and orthopnea, with jugular venous distention, hepatomegaly, edema, and pulsus paradoxus (classic specific signs like friction rub are less common than in benign disease).
Echocardiography is the most helpful diagnostic test, and cytology of pericardial fluid is diagnostic in most patients (tumor markers in the fluid are not helpful).
Acute tamponade with hemodynamic instability requires immediate drainage by pericardiocentesis; recurrence after percutaneous drainage is ~20% and may be reduced by sclerotherapy or a pericardial window.
keyPoints: Echocardiography is the key test; pericardial-fluid cytology is usually diagnostic
Tamponade with instability → immediate pericardiocentesis
Recurrence ~20% after drainage; reduce with sclerotherapy/pericardial window
items.reference: PERICARDIAL EFFUSION/TAMPONADE: "Echocardiography is the most helpful diagnostic test."

items.title: How to recognize and urgently treat malignant spinal cord compression
items.content:
Spinal cord compression is a true emergency where speed preserves function.
It occurs in 5–10% of cancer patients (lung, breast, prostate cancers and myeloma most often), most commonly in the thoracic spine (70%); localized back pain and tenderness is the most common and usually earliest symptom — present for days to months before neurologic deficits — and it characteristically worsens when supine (unlike disk disease).
Lhermitte's sign may be an early feature, and loss of bowel/bladder control usually occurs late; treatment is more often successful when the patient is still ambulatory with sphincter control.
A cancer patient with back pain suspicious for compression should receive dexamethasone immediately and undergo MRI — do not wait.
keyPoints: Back pain (worse supine) precedes deficits; bowel/bladder loss is late
Thoracic spine most common (70%); lung/breast/prostate/myeloma
Start dexamethasone IMMEDIATELY and get urgent MRI; outcome best while ambulatory with sphincter control
items.reference: SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION: "Those whose physical examination suggests cord compression should receive dexamethasone starting immediately and undergo MRI imaging."

items.title: Why MRI is the imaging procedure of choice for cord compression
items.content:
Imaging must visualize the whole cord and not be misled by plain-film findings.
The earliest plain-film sign of vertebral tumor is pedicle erosion (the 'winking owl' sign), but plain films are insensitive and a normal film does not exclude cancer; importantly, ~20% of vertebral collapse (especially in older/postmenopausal women) is due to osteoporosis, not tumor.
MRI provides a full-length image of the cord and is the imaging procedure of choice, being highly sensitive to marrow replacement by tumor and able to show intraspinal extradural masses; multiple epidural metastases are present in 25% of patients and change management.
Myelography (± CT) is reserved for patients who cannot undergo MRI promptly.
keyPoints: 'Winking owl' (pedicle erosion) is earliest plain-film sign, but films are insensitive
~20% of vertebral collapse is osteoporotic, not malignant
MRI is the procedure of choice (full-length cord; detects 25% multiple epidural mets)
items.reference: SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION: "The full-length image of the cord provided by MRI is the imaging procedure of choice."

items.title: How the NOMS framework guides cord-compression treatment
items.content:
Treatment aims to relieve pain and preserve/restore neurologic function, using a structured assessment.
Radiation therapy plus glucocorticoids is generally the initial treatment of choice for most patients, and the NOMS framework (Neurologic, Oncologic, Mechanical, Systemic) developed at MSKCC guides decisions.
For radioresistant tumors, stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) is preferred, often after 'separation surgery' (circumferential excision to give a safe 2-mm margin from the cord) in high-grade compression.
A surgical approach is the treatment of choice when there is mechanical instability or retropulsion of bone into the canal; bisphosphonates and/or denosumab help prevent compression in patients with bony involvement.
keyPoints: Default: radiation + glucocorticoids; decide via NOMS (Neurologic/Oncologic/Mechanical/Systemic)
Radioresistant tumor → SBRT (± separation surgery for high-grade compression)
Surgery first for mechanical instability/bone retropulsion; bisphosphonates/denosumab for prevention
items.reference: TREATMENT (Spinal Cord Compression): "Radiation therapy plus glucocorticoids is generally the initial treatment of choice for most patients with SCC."

items.title: How brain metastases and raised intracranial pressure are managed
items.content:
Brain metastases cause major morbidity and the initial treatment is steroids.
Lung and breast cancers and melanoma metastasize to brain most often; presentations include headache, nausea/vomiting, behavioral change, seizures, and focal deficits, and an abrupt stroke-like onset usually reflects hemorrhage into the metastasis (melanoma, germ cell, and renal cell cancers bleed especially often).
Gadolinium-enhanced MRI is superior to CT and shows multiple enhancing lesions with surrounding low-density edema.
Dexamethasone is the best initial treatment for all symptomatic patients with brain metastases; stereotactic radiosurgery is favored for a limited number (1–4) of metastases, increasingly chosen over whole-brain radiation because of cognitive toxicity.
keyPoints: Lung/breast cancer and melanoma metastasize to brain; sudden onset suggests hemorrhage (melanoma/germ cell/RCC)
Gadolinium MRI > CT; dexamethasone is the best initial treatment for symptomatic brain metastases
SRS preferred for 1–4 metastases; WBRT causes cognitive dysfunction
items.reference: TREATMENT (Increased Intracranial Pressure): "Dexamethasone is the best initial treatment for all symptomatic patients with brain metastases."

items.title: Why a single negative CSF cytology does not exclude neoplastic meningitis
items.content:
Leptomeningeal disease produces multifocal neurology and is hard to confirm.
Melanoma, breast and lung cancer, lymphoma, and acute leukemia are the most common causes; patients present with multifocal signs (headache, cranial nerve palsies, gait abnormality, radicular pain, limb weakness).
Diagnosis requires malignant cells in CSF, but up to 40% of patients have false-negative cytology, so a patient with consistent signs and negative cytology should have the lumbar puncture repeated at least once; an elevated CSF protein is nearly always present.
Treatment is intrathecal chemotherapy (methotrexate, cytarabine, or thiotepa) ± systemic therapy and focal radiation; prognosis is poor (median survival 10–12 weeks), though leukemia/lymphoma may be cured if systemic disease is controlled.
keyPoints: Up to 40% false-negative CSF cytology → repeat LP if clinically suspected
Elevated CSF protein nearly always present; MRI shows leptomeningeal enhancement
Intrathecal chemo (MTX/cytarabine/thiotepa); poor prognosis (~10–12 weeks)
items.reference: NEOPLASTIC MENINGITIS: "up to 40% of patients may have false-negative CSF cytology...should have the spinal tap repeated at least one more time for cytologic examination."

items.title: How to choose an anticonvulsant in a cancer patient (and why)
items.content:
Anticonvulsant choice in oncology hinges on drug interactions.
CNS metastases are the most common cause of seizures in cancer patients, though seizures are more frequent with primary brain tumors; prophylactic anticonvulsant therapy is not recommended.
Patients with documented seizures from CNS metastases should receive levetiracetam, lacosamide, or lamotrigine — agents not metabolized by hepatic CYP450 — because enzyme-inducing drugs like phenytoin alter the metabolism of many antitumor agents (irinotecan, taxanes, etoposide, imatinib, sorafenib, sunitinib, everolimus, vemurafenib, and others).
Levetiracetam has become the preferred drug.
keyPoints: CNS metastases are the commonest cause of seizures; do not give prophylactic anticonvulsants
Use non-CYP450 agents (levetiracetam preferred, lacosamide, lamotrigine)
Avoid CYP450 inducers (e.g., phenytoin) that alter chemotherapy/targeted-agent metabolism
items.reference: TREATMENT (Seizures): "Levetiracetam, lacosamide, and lamotrigine are anticonvulsant agents not metabolized by the hepatic CYP450 system...Levetiracetam has become the preferred drug."

items.title: Why hyperleukocytosis with leukostasis is an emergency
items.content:
Very high blast counts cause vascular sludging in brain and lung.
Leukostasis can occur in acute leukemia (especially myeloid) when the peripheral blast count exceeds ~100,000/µL: increased blood viscosity and aggregates slow flow, and primitive myeloid blasts invade the endothelium causing hemorrhage, affecting brain and lung most.
Brain leukostasis causes stupor, headache, visual disturbance, and possible sudden death; pulmonary leukostasis causes hypoxemic respiratory distress.
Hydroxyurea can rapidly lower the count while the workup proceeds, leukapheresis is used for symptomatic patients, and induction chemotherapy should start quickly; note that pulse oximetry (not arterial blood gas) is the accurate way to assess oxygenation, because white cells consume oxygen in the sample causing spuriously low PaO2.
keyPoints: Blast count >100,000/µL (myeloid leukemia) → viscosity, sludging, endothelial invasion (brain/lung)
Treat with hydroxyurea, leukapheresis (if symptomatic), and prompt induction chemotherapy
Use pulse oximetry, not ABG (leukocytes consume O2 → spuriously low PaO2); avoid raising viscosity
items.reference: PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS: "Pulse oximetry is the most accurate way of assessing oxygenation in patients with hyperleukocytosis."

items.title: How tumor lysis syndrome is defined and recognized
items.content:
TLS is the metabolic storm of massive tumor-cell destruction.
It is characterized by hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hypocalcemia (with frequent acute renal failure and possible acidosis), caused by destruction of large numbers of rapidly proliferating cells.
It is most often associated with Burkitt's lymphoma, acute lymphoblastic leukemia, and AML (and is increased with venetoclax in lymphoid neoplasms), usually occurring during or 1–5 days after chemotherapy.
Risk correlates with tumor burden — hyperuricemia and a high LDH (>1500 U/L) both reflect burden and predict TLS; note that high leukocyte/platelet counts can cause pseudohyperkalemia (no ECG changes), in which plasma rather than serum potassium should be followed.
keyPoints: TLS tetrad: hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia ± acute renal failure
Highest risk: Burkitt lymphoma, ALL, AML (and venetoclax); occurs during/1–5 days after chemo
LDH >1500 U/L predicts risk; pseudohyperkalemia (no ECG changes) → follow plasma K+
items.reference: TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME: "Tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) is characterized by hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hypocalcemia and is caused by the destruction of a large number of rapidly proliferating neoplastic cells."

items.title: How to prevent and treat tumor lysis syndrome
items.content:
Recognition of risk and prevention are the most important steps.
The standard preventive approach is allopurinol plus aggressive hydration; urinary alkalinization with sodium bicarbonate is no longer recommended because, although it raises uric-acid solubility, a high pH lowers solubility of xanthine and calcium phosphate, promoting intratubular crystallization.
Febuxostat is an alternative xanthine oxidase inhibitor (useful in allopurinol allergy/renal failure), and rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) converts uric acid to soluble allantoin, acts within hours, and is used for high-risk prophylaxis or when uric acid cannot be controlled (especially with renal failure).
Renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis preferred) is often needed and should be considered early.
keyPoints: Prevention = allopurinol + aggressive hydration; sodium bicarbonate alkalinization is NO LONGER recommended
Febuxostat for allopurinol allergy/renal failure; rasburicase for high risk or uncontrolled uric acid
Early hemodialysis for oliguric/anuric renal failure
items.reference: TREATMENT (Tumor Lysis Syndrome): "The standard preventive approach consists of allopurinol and aggressive hydration. Urinary alkalization with sodium bicarbonate is no longer recommended."

items.title: Why rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency
items.content:
Rasburicase's mechanism creates a hazard in a specific deficiency.
Rasburicase is recombinant urate oxidase, an enzyme missing in primates, that converts poorly soluble uric acid to readily soluble allantoin — and the urate-oxidase reaction generates hydrogen peroxide as an end product.
It is contraindicated in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency, who cannot break down hydrogen peroxide (risking hemolysis and methemoglobinemia).
A practical pitfall: rasburicase continues to degrade uric acid ex vivo in the test tube at room temperature, causing spuriously low uric acid readings — samples must be cooled immediately to deactivate the enzyme.
keyPoints: Rasburicase converts uric acid → allantoin, producing hydrogen peroxide
Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (cannot detoxify H2O2 → hemolysis/methemoglobinemia)
Causes spuriously low uric acid ex vivo — cool the sample immediately
items.reference: TREATMENT (Tumor Lysis Syndrome): "Rasburicase is contraindicated in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency who are unable to break down hydrogen peroxide, an end product of the urate oxidase reaction."

items.title: How cytokine release syndrome from antibodies and CAR-T is treated
items.content:
Immune-effector therapies can trigger a systemic inflammatory syndrome.
Initial infusion of human/humanized antibodies (rituximab, trastuzumab, alemtuzumab, blinatumomab, and others) causes fever, chills, and headache in up to half of patients, with bronchospasm/hypotension in ~1%; the pathogenesis is cytokine release syndrome (CRS) from activation of immune effector cells and release of TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-6, and IL-10.
The most common acute toxicity of CAR-T-cell therapy is CRS, which can be associated with cardiac dysfunction and neurotoxicity, and macrophage activation syndrome/HLH can complicate severe CRS.
Tocilizumab, which blocks IL-6 binding to its receptor, is an effective treatment for severe or life-threatening CRS, alongside supportive care.
keyPoints: CRS = cytokine release (TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-6, IL-10) from antibodies or CAR-T cells
Most common acute CAR-T toxicity; can cause cardiac dysfunction, neurotoxicity, HLH/MAS
Treat severe/life-threatening CRS with tocilizumab (anti-IL-6 receptor) + supportive care
items.reference: HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS: "tocilizumab is an effective treatment for severe or life-threatening CRS."

items.title: Why hemorrhagic cystitis occurs and how mesna prevents it
items.content:
A toxic urinary metabolite injures the bladder, and prevention is key.
Hemorrhagic cystitis develops from chemotherapy (mostly cyclophosphamide or ifosfamide), radiation, transplantation, or opportunistic infection; both cyclophosphamide and ifosfamide are metabolized to acrolein, a strong chemical irritant excreted in the urine that causes bladder irritation and hemorrhage.
The best management is prevention — maintaining a high urine flow minimizes exposure, and 2-mercaptoethanesulfonate (mesna) detoxifies the metabolites and is co-administered (usually three doses, each 20% of the ifosfamide dose).
If cystitis develops, high urine flow may suffice; refractory cases may need bladder irrigation (alum or formalin), and in the transplant setting late-onset cystitis is often due to BK polyomavirus or adenovirus.
keyPoints: Cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide → acrolein → hemorrhagic cystitis
Prevent with high urine flow + mesna (detoxifies the metabolite)
Refractory: bladder irrigation (alum/formalin); post-transplant late onset often BK virus/adenovirus
items.reference: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS: "2-mercaptoethanesulfonate (mesna) detoxifies the metabolites and can be coadministered with the instigating drugs."

items.title: How malignant intestinal obstruction is managed
items.content:
Obstruction in advanced cancer is often managed conservatively and palliatively.
It is common in colorectal and ovarian cancer (peritoneal carcinomatosis typically obstructs at multiple sites), and pseudo-obstruction can result from mesenteric/celiac-plexus involvement or paraneoplastic neuropathy (small-cell lung cancer); CT differentiates malignant (mass, abrupt transition, irregular thickening) from benign (adhesions, smooth transition) causes, and ~25–30% of obstructions in cancer patients are benign (often adhesions).
Management depends on disease extent and prognosis (median survival 3–4 months); surgery carries 10–20% mortality, so patients with advanced intra-abdominal malignancy often get prolonged conservative management with nasogastric decompression.
Self-expanding stents, a venting gastrostomy, octreotide (reduces GI secretions), and glucocorticoids help palliate symptoms and may keep patients out of hospital. Acute cecal dilation >12–14 cm is a surgical emergency (rupture risk).
keyPoints: Colorectal/ovarian cancer; ~25–30% of obstructions are benign (adhesions); surgery mortality 10–20%
Conservative care (NG decompression), stents, venting gastrostomy, octreotide, glucocorticoids
Cecal dilation >12–14 cm = surgical emergency (rupture)
items.reference: TREATMENT (Intestinal Obstruction): "Octreotide may relieve obstructive symptoms through its inhibitory effect on gastrointestinal secretion."

items.title: How urinary and biliary obstruction are relieved
items.content:
Both obstructions are relieved by decompression, guided by symptoms and prognosis.
Urinary obstruction (prostate, cervical, or other malignancies; pelvic radiation fibrosis) presents with flank pain or a slow creatinine rise; renal ultrasound is the safest, cheapest way to detect hydronephrosis, and obstruction with flank pain, sepsis, or fistula is an indication for immediate diversion by internal ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy (bladder outlet obstruction → suprapubic cystostomy).
Malignant biliary obstruction (pancreas, ampulla, bile duct, or metastases) causes painless jaundice, pale stools, dark urine, and pruritus; palliative intervention is indicated for disabling pruritus, severe malabsorption, or infection, using stenting (radiographic/endoscopic), surgical bypass, or radiation ± chemotherapy.
In both, aggressive invasive intervention must be weighed against likely antitumor response and reversibility of organ dysfunction.
keyPoints: Urinary obstruction: ultrasound for hydronephrosis; diversion (ureteral stent/nephrostomy) for pain/sepsis/fistula
Malignant biliary obstruction: painless jaundice/pruritus → stent, bypass, or radiation ± chemo
Weigh invasive intervention against antitumor response and reversibility
items.reference: TREATMENT (Urinary Obstruction): "Obstruction associated with flank pain, sepsis, or fistula formation is an indication for immediate palliative urinary diversion."

items.title: How massive hemoptysis is managed emergently
items.content:
Massive hemoptysis kills by asphyxiation, so the airway comes first.
Massive hemoptysis is >200–600 mL in 24 h, but any hemoptysis threatening life should be treated as massive; lung cancer accounts for a large proportion of cases.
The first priorities are to maintain the airway, optimize oxygenation, and stabilize hemodynamics — if the bleeding side is known, place the patient bleeding-side-down to protect the unaffected lung; if bleeding continues or the airway is compromised, intubate and perform emergency bronchoscopy.
Massive hemoptysis usually originates from the high-pressure bronchial circulation, and bronchial artery embolization is the first-line definitive procedure (controls bleeding in 75–90%); note that anti-VEGF antibodies (bevacizumab, ramucirumab) are associated with life-threatening hemoptysis in squamous NSCLC.
keyPoints: Massive hemoptysis >200–600 mL/24 h (or any life-threatening volume); airway/oxygenation/hemodynamics first
Bleeding side DOWN to protect the good lung; intubate + bronchoscopy if uncontrolled
Bronchial artery embolization is first-line definitive therapy (controls 75–90%)
items.reference: HEMOPTYSIS: "Bronchial artery embolization is considered a first-line definitive procedure for managing hemoptysis."

items.title: How non-islet tumors cause hypoglycemia via IGF-II
items.content:
Large non-pancreatic tumors can cause hypoglycemia through a growth-factor mechanism.
Persistent hypoglycemia occurs occasionally with large tumors of mesenchymal origin (retroperitoneum/thorax), hepatomas, or adrenocortical tumors, causing obtundation, confusion, and behavioral changes in the postabsorptive state.
These tumors often secrete incompletely processed insulin-like growth factor II ('big IGF-II'), which activates insulin receptors; the biochemical signature is an increased IGF-II to IGF-I ratio with suppressed insulin and C-peptide and inappropriately low growth hormone and β-hydroxybutyrate.
If the tumor cannot be resected, symptoms may be relieved by glucose, glucocorticoids, recombinant growth hormone, or glucagon. Beware artifactual pseudohypoglycemia from in-vitro glucose consumption by very high white-cell counts.
keyPoints: Large mesenchymal/hepatic/adrenocortical tumors secrete 'big IGF-II' → activates insulin receptors
Pattern: high IGF-II:IGF-I, suppressed insulin/C-peptide, low GH and β-hydroxybutyrate
Treat with glucose/glucocorticoids/GH/glucagon if unresectable; hyperleukocytosis can cause pseudohypoglycemia
items.reference: HYPOGLYCEMIA: "These tumors often secrete incompletely processed insulin-like growth factor II (IGF-II), a hormone capable of activating insulin receptors and causing hypoglycemia."

items.title: Why adrenal insufficiency is easily missed in cancer patients
items.content:
Nonspecific symptoms and multiple drug causes obscure a potentially lethal diagnosis.
Adrenal insufficiency may go unrecognized because nausea, vomiting, anorexia, and orthostatic hypotension are easily attributed to progressive cancer or therapy; causes include bilateral adrenal metastases or hemorrhage, adrenalectomy, and drugs (mitotane, ketoconazole, aminoglutethimide, rapid steroid taper).
Megestrol acetate (used for cachexia) suppresses cortisol/ACTH and can cause adrenal insufficiency (paradoxically, also Cushing's/hyperglycemia from its glucocorticoid-like activity).
Immune checkpoint inhibitors cause autoimmune hypophysitis (ipilimumab/anti-CTLA-4, typically 6–12 weeks after starting, → secondary adrenal insufficiency, often permanent) and autoimmune adrenalitis; acute adrenal crisis is potentially lethal, and treatment is started after sampling cortisol and ACTH.
keyPoints: Nonspecific symptoms easily misattributed; causes include adrenal metastases/hemorrhage and drugs
Megestrol suppresses cortisol/ACTH; checkpoint inhibitors cause hypophysitis (ipilimumab ~6–12 wk) and adrenalitis
Adrenal crisis is lethal — treat after sampling cortisol/ACTH
items.reference: ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY: "adrenal insufficiency may go unrecognized because the symptoms, such as nausea, vomiting, anorexia, and orthostatic hypotension, are nonspecific and may be mistakenly attributed to progressive cancer or to therapy."

items.question: Which malignancy accounts for roughly 85% of malignant superior vena cava syndrome?
items.explanation: Lung cancer, particularly small-cell and squamous histologies, accounts for ~85% of malignant SVCS; lymphoma is a leading cause in young adults, and intravascular devices now cause at least 40% of (benign) cases.
items.reference: SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME: "Lung cancer, particularly of small-cell and squamous cell histologies, accounts for ~85% of all cases of malignant origin."

items.question: What is the one potentially life-threatening complication of a superior mediastinal mass causing SVCS?
items.explanation: Tracheal obstruction is the one potentially life-threatening complication, and upper airway obstruction demands emergent therapy. Cerebral or laryngeal edema also signals a poorer prognosis.
items.reference: TREATMENT (SVCS): "The one potentially life-threatening complication of a superior mediastinal mass is tracheal obstruction."

items.question: What is the most helpful diagnostic test for malignant pericardial effusion/tamponade?
items.explanation: Echocardiography is the most helpful diagnostic test; cytology of pericardial fluid is diagnostic in most patients, whereas tumor markers in the fluid are not helpful.
items.reference: PERICARDIAL EFFUSION/TAMPONADE: "Echocardiography is the most helpful diagnostic test."

items.question: A cancer patient has back pain and an examination suggesting cord compression. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Await routine MRI scheduled next week
Start dexamethasone immediately and obtain urgent MRI
Order plain spine films only and observe
items.explanation: Patients whose examination suggests cord compression should receive dexamethasone immediately and undergo MRI. Treatment is more successful when patients are still ambulatory with sphincter control.
items.reference: SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION: "Those whose physical examination suggests cord compression should receive dexamethasone starting immediately and undergo MRI imaging."

items.question: Which feature helps distinguish the back pain of spinal cord compression from that of disk disease?
options: It worsens when the patient is supine
It is never exacerbated by coughing
items.explanation: The localized back pain of cord compression characteristically worsens when the patient is supine (and with movement, coughing, or sneezing), unlike the pain of disk disease.
items.reference: SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION: "It can be differentiated from the pain of disk disease by the fact that it worsens when the patient is supine."

items.question: A cancer patient has a vertebral compression fracture on plain film. What proportion of vertebral collapse (especially in older/postmenopausal women) is due to osteoporosis rather than tumor?
items.explanation: About 20% of vertebral collapse — particularly in older patients and postmenopausal women — is due to osteoporosis, not cancer; vertebral collapse is therefore not a reliable indicator of tumor, and MRI is the imaging procedure of choice.
items.reference: SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION: "~20% of cases of vertebral collapse, particularly those in older patients and postmenopausal women, are due not to cancer but to osteoporosis."

items.question: What is the best initial treatment for a symptomatic patient with brain metastases and raised intracranial pressure?
items.explanation: Dexamethasone is the best initial treatment for all symptomatic patients with brain metastases. Stereotactic radiosurgery is favored for 1–4 metastases; lumbar puncture is contraindicated with a mass and raised ICP.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Increased Intracranial Pressure): "Dexamethasone is the best initial treatment for all symptomatic patients with brain metastases."

items.question: A sudden stroke-like onset of headache and deficit from a brain metastasis is most often due to hemorrhage. Which cancers have a particularly high incidence of intracranial bleeding?
options: Melanoma, germ cell tumors, and renal cell cancer
items.explanation: Melanoma, germ cell tumors, and renal cell cancers have a particularly high incidence of intracranial bleeding, which can present as a sudden stroke-like deterioration due to hemorrhage into the metastasis.
items.reference: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE: "Melanoma, germ cell tumors, and renal cell cancers have a particularly high incidence of intracranial bleeding."

items.question: A patient with multifocal cranial neuropathies has a single negative CSF cytology for neoplastic meningitis. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat the lumbar puncture for cytology at least once
items.explanation: Up to 40% of patients have false-negative CSF cytology, so a patient with consistent signs and a negative cytology should have the lumbar puncture repeated at least once. An elevated CSF protein is nearly always present.
items.reference: NEOPLASTIC MENINGITIS: "Patients with neurologic signs and symptoms consistent with neoplastic meningitis who have a negative CSF cytology should have the spinal tap repeated at least one more time..."

items.question: Why is levetiracetam preferred over phenytoin for seizures in a patient receiving chemotherapy?
options: It is not metabolized by hepatic CYP450, so it does not alter antitumor drug metabolism
It can be given only intravenously
items.explanation: Levetiracetam (and lacosamide, lamotrigine) are not metabolized by hepatic CYP450 and do not alter antitumor drug metabolism, unlike enzyme-inducing agents like phenytoin that affect many chemotherapy and targeted agents. Levetiracetam is the preferred drug.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Seizures): "Levetiracetam, lacosamide, and lamotrigine are anticonvulsant agents not metabolized by the hepatic CYP450 system and do not alter the metabolism of antitumor agents."

items.question: In a patient with AML and a blast count of 200,000/µL with respiratory distress, how should oxygenation be most accurately assessed?
items.explanation: In hyperleukocytosis, the markedly increased white cells consume plasma oxygen in the sample, causing a spuriously low arterial PaO2; pulse oximetry is the most accurate way to assess oxygenation.
items.reference: PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS: "Pulse oximetry is the most accurate way of assessing oxygenation in patients with hyperleukocytosis."

items.question: Which set of metabolic abnormalities characterizes tumor lysis syndrome?
items.explanation: TLS is characterized by hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hypocalcemia (reciprocal to the high phosphate), with frequent acute renal failure.
items.reference: TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME: "Tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) is characterized by hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hypocalcemia..."

items.question: Which statement about preventing tumor lysis syndrome is correct?
options: Urinary alkalinization with sodium bicarbonate is now strongly recommended
The standard preventive approach is allopurinol plus aggressive hydration
items.explanation: The standard preventive approach is allopurinol plus aggressive hydration. Urinary alkalinization with sodium bicarbonate is no longer recommended because high pH decreases the solubility of xanthine and calcium phosphate, promoting crystallization.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Tumor Lysis Syndrome): "The standard preventive approach consists of allopurinol and aggressive hydration. Urinary alkalization with sodium bicarbonate is no longer recommended."

items.question: Rasburicase is contraindicated in patients with which condition?
items.explanation: Rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency because patients cannot break down the hydrogen peroxide produced by the urate-oxidase reaction, risking hemolysis. (It is actually preferred when renal failure is present.)
items.reference: TREATMENT (Tumor Lysis Syndrome): "Rasburicase is contraindicated in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency who are unable to break down hydrogen peroxide..."

items.question: A patient develops severe cytokine release syndrome after CAR-T-cell therapy. Which targeted treatment is effective?
items.explanation: Tocilizumab, which blocks IL-6 binding to its receptor, is an effective treatment for severe or life-threatening CRS, along with supportive care. CRS is the most common acute toxicity of CAR-T therapy.
items.reference: HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS: "tocilizumab is an effective treatment for severe or life-threatening CRS."

items.question: Which agent is co-administered with ifosfamide to prevent hemorrhagic cystitis by detoxifying acrolein?
items.explanation: Mesna detoxifies the acrolein metabolite of cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide and is co-administered (usually three doses, each 20% of the ifosfamide dose), along with maintaining high urine flow.
items.reference: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS: "2-mercaptoethanesulfonate (mesna) detoxifies the metabolites and can be coadministered with the instigating drugs."

items.question: Which agent relieves malignant bowel-obstruction symptoms through its inhibitory effect on gastrointestinal secretion?
items.explanation: Octreotide may relieve obstructive symptoms through its inhibitory effect on gastrointestinal secretion; conservative management also includes NG decompression, antiemetics, and sometimes a venting gastrostomy.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Intestinal Obstruction): "Octreotide may relieve obstructive symptoms through its inhibitory effect on gastrointestinal secretion."

items.question: Which is considered the first-line definitive procedure for managing massive hemoptysis?
items.explanation: Massive hemoptysis usually arises from the high-pressure bronchial circulation; bronchial artery embolization is the first-line definitive procedure, controlling bleeding in 75–90% of patients.
items.reference: HEMOPTYSIS: "Bronchial artery embolization is considered a first-line definitive procedure for managing hemoptysis."

items.question: A patient with a large retroperitoneal mesenchymal tumor has recurrent hypoglycemia with suppressed insulin and C-peptide. The most likely mediator is:
options: Incompletely processed insulin-like growth factor II (big IGF-II)
items.explanation: Large mesenchymal (or hepatic/adrenocortical) tumors often secrete incompletely processed IGF-II ('big IGF-II'), which activates insulin receptors; the pattern is a high IGF-II:IGF-I ratio with suppressed insulin and C-peptide.
items.reference: HYPOGLYCEMIA: "These tumors often secrete incompletely processed insulin-like growth factor II (IGF-II), a hormone capable of activating insulin receptors and causing hypoglycemia."

items.question: Ipilimumab-associated autoimmune hypophysitis leading to secondary adrenal insufficiency typically occurs at what time after starting therapy?
items.explanation: Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) hypophysitis symptoms occur at an average of 6–12 weeks after initiation; it can cause hypopituitarism including secondary adrenal insufficiency (often permanent) — treated with early glucocorticoids and hormone replacement.
items.reference: ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY: "Ipilimumab-associated hypophysitis symptoms occur at an average of 6–12 weeks after initiation of therapy."

items.statement: A normal chest radiograph excludes the diagnosis of superior vena cava syndrome.
items.explanation: False — SVCS is a clinical diagnosis; a normal chest radiograph is still compatible with the diagnosis if other characteristic findings are present, and CT best shows the mediastinal anatomy.
items.reference: SUPERIOR VENA CAVA SYNDROME: "a normal chest radiograph is still compatible with the diagnosis if other characteristic findings are present."

items.statement: Chemotherapy is the most effective treatment for SVCS caused by small-cell lung cancer, lymphoma, or germ cell tumor.
items.explanation: True — chemotherapy is effective when the underlying cancer is small-cell lung carcinoma, lymphoma, or germ cell tumor, whereas radiation is primary for non-small-cell lung cancer and other solid tumors.
items.reference: TREATMENT (SVCS): "Chemotherapy is effective when the underlying cancer is small-cell carcinoma of the lung, lymphoma, or germ cell tumor."

items.statement: The thoracic spine is the most common site of malignant spinal cord compression.
items.explanation: True — the thoracic spine is the most common site (70%), followed by the lumbosacral (20%) and cervical (10%) spine.
items.reference: SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION: "The thoracic spine is the most common site (70%), followed by the lumbosacral spine (20%) and the cervical spine (10%)."

items.statement: Prophylactic anticonvulsant therapy is recommended for all patients with brain metastases.
items.explanation: False — prophylactic anticonvulsant therapy is not recommended; anticonvulsants (preferably levetiracetam) are given to patients with documented seizures.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Seizures): "Prophylactic anticonvulsant therapy is not recommended."

items.statement: Leukostasis syndrome can occur in acute (especially myeloid) leukemia when the peripheral blast count exceeds about 100,000/µL.
items.explanation: True — hyperleukocytosis and leukostasis can occur when the peripheral blast cell count is >100,000/µL, with brain and lung most commonly affected; it is rare in chronic leukemias.
items.reference: PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS: "can occur when the peripheral blast cell count is >100,000/mL."

items.statement: An elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH >1500 U/L) correlates with tumor burden and the risk of tumor lysis syndrome.
items.explanation: True — hyperuricemia and high LDH (>1500 U/L) both correlate with total tumor burden and with the risk of TLS.
items.reference: TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME: "Hyperuricemia and high serum levels of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH >1500 U/L), both of which correlate with total tumor burden, also correlate with the risk of TLS."

items.statement: High leukocyte and platelet counts can cause pseudohyperkalemia, in which no electrocardiographic abnormalities are present.
items.explanation: True — high leukocyte/platelet counts can artificially elevate measured potassium (pseudohyperkalemia) due to cell lysis after the blood is drawn; there are no ECG abnormalities, and plasma (not serum) potassium should be followed.
items.reference: TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME: "High leukocyte and platelet counts may artificially elevate potassium levels (\"pseudohyperkalemia\")...In pseudohyperkalemia, no electrocardiographic abnormalities are present."

items.statement: Rasburicase can cause spuriously low uric acid readings because it continues to degrade uric acid in the test tube at room temperature.
items.explanation: True — rasburicase causes ex vivo enzymatic degradation of uric acid at room temperature, leading to spuriously low levels; samples must be cooled immediately to deactivate the enzyme.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Tumor Lysis Syndrome): "This leads to spurious low uric acid levels during laboratory monitoring of the patient with TLS. Samples must be cooled immediately to deactivate the urate oxidase."

items.statement: All intestinal obstruction in cancer patients is due to the malignancy itself.
items.explanation: False — about 25–30% of intestinal obstructions in cancer patients are due to causes other than cancer, with adhesions from previous operations a common benign cause (and drug-induced ileus another reversible cause).
items.reference: INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION: "About 25–30% of patients are found to have intestinal obstruction due to causes other than cancer."

items.statement: Anti-VEGF antibodies such as bevacizumab have been associated with life-threatening hemoptysis in squamous non-small-cell lung cancer.
items.explanation: True — VEGF-inhibiting antibodies (bevacizumab, ramucirumab) have been associated with life-threatening hemoptysis in NSCLC, particularly squamous histology; cavitary lesions or recent hemoptysis raise the risk.
items.reference: HEMOPTYSIS: "Antibodies to vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) including bevacizumab and ramucirumab...have been associated with life-threatening hemoptysis in patients with non-small-cell lung cancer, particularly of squamous cell histology."

items.statement: Megestrol acetate, used for cancer cachexia, can suppress cortisol and ACTH and cause adrenal insufficiency.
items.explanation: True — megestrol acetate may suppress plasma cortisol and ACTH and cause adrenal insufficiency (and, paradoxically, sometimes Cushing's syndrome/hyperglycemia from its glucocorticoid-like activity).
items.reference: ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY: "Megestrol acetate, used to manage cancer and HIV-related cachexia, may suppress plasma levels of cortisol and adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)."

items.statement: Pneumatosis intestinalis is a specific CT finding seen in neutropenic enterocolitis with ischemia.
items.explanation: True — pneumatosis intestinalis is a more specific finding seen only in those with neutropenic enterocolitis and ischemia; combined small- and large-bowel involvement also suggests the diagnosis.
items.reference: NEUTROPENIC ENTEROCOLITIS: "Pneumatosis intestinalis is a more specific finding, seen only in those with neutropenic enterocolitis and ischemia."

items.assertion: A cancer patient with new back pain and suspected cord compression should receive dexamethasone before MRI is completed.
items.reason: Treatment outcomes are better when therapy is started while the patient is still ambulatory and has sphincter control.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because function is preserved best when treatment begins early, dexamethasone is started immediately (not delayed for imaging) when compression is suspected.
items.reference: SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION: "Treatment is more often successful in patients who are ambulatory and still have sphincter control at the time treatment is initiated."

items.assertion: Arterial blood gas PaO2 should not be relied upon to assess oxygenation in a patient with hyperleukocytosis.
items.reason: The markedly increased white blood cells rapidly consume plasma oxygen in the sample, causing a spuriously low arterial oxygen tension.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: in-vitro oxygen consumption by excess white cells gives a falsely low PaO2, so pulse oximetry is preferred.
items.reference: PULMONARY AND INTRACEREBRAL LEUKOSTASIS: "Rapid consumption of plasma oxygen by the markedly increased number of white blood cells can cause spuriously low arterial oxygen tension."

items.assertion: Routine urinary alkalinization with sodium bicarbonate should be used to prevent tumor lysis syndrome.
items.reason: A high urine pH increases the solubility of xanthine, hypoxanthine, and calcium phosphate.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Sodium bicarbonate alkalinization is no longer recommended because a high pH actually DECREASES the solubility of xanthine, hypoxanthine, and calcium phosphate, promoting crystallization. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Tumor Lysis Syndrome): "a high pH decreases the solubility of xanthine, hypoxanthine, and calcium phosphate, potentially increasing the likelihood of intratubular crystallization."

items.assertion: Rasburicase should be avoided in patients with G6PD deficiency.
items.reason: The urate-oxidase reaction generates hydrogen peroxide, which G6PD-deficient patients cannot detoxify.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because G6PD-deficient patients cannot break down the hydrogen peroxide produced by urate oxidase, rasburicase risks hemolysis and is contraindicated.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Tumor Lysis Syndrome): "Rasburicase is contraindicated in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency who are unable to break down hydrogen peroxide, an end product of the urate oxidase reaction."

items.assertion: A normal plain radiograph of the spine excludes malignant cord compression.
items.reason: Plain films are insensitive, and the earliest sign (pedicle erosion, the 'winking owl' sign) is easily missed.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — a normal plain film does not exclude cancer, and MRI is the procedure of choice. The reason is true (plain films are insensitive) and explains why the assertion fails. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION: "a normal appearance on plain films of the spine does not exclude the diagnosis of cancer."

items.assertion: A patient with massive hemoptysis from a known left-sided lesion should be positioned with the left side down.
items.reason: Positioning the bleeding side down protects the unaffected lung from aspiration of blood.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: placing the bleeding side down prevents blood from spilling into and flooding the healthy lung, protecting gas exchange.
items.reference: HEMOPTYSIS: "the patient should be placed in a lateral decubitus position, with the bleeding side down to prevent aspiration into the unaffected lung..."

items.assertion: Phenytoin is best avoided when treating seizures in a patient on targeted anticancer therapy.
items.reason: Phenytoin induces hepatic CYP450, altering the metabolism of many antitumor agents such as imatinib, sorafenib, and everolimus.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because CYP450 induction by phenytoin changes the levels of many antitumor drugs, non-inducing agents like levetiracetam are preferred.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Seizures): "Most antiseizure medications including phenytoin induce cytochrome P450 (CYP450), which alters the metabolism of many antitumor agents..."

items.assertion: Maintaining a high urine flow rate helps prevent cyclophosphamide- and ifosfamide-induced hemorrhagic cystitis.
items.reason: High urine flow minimizes the bladder mucosa's exposure to the irritant metabolite acrolein.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because acrolein excreted in urine causes the injury, diluting and flushing it with high urine flow (plus mesna) reduces bladder exposure.
items.reference: HEMORRHAGIC CYSTITIS: "Maintaining a high rate of urine flow minimizes exposure."

items.assertion: Tumor markers measured in pericardial fluid are useful for diagnosing malignant pericardial effusion.
items.reason: Cytologic examination of pericardial fluid is diagnostic in most patients with malignant pericardial disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — measurements of tumor markers in pericardial fluid are not helpful. The reason is true (cytology is diagnostic in most patients) and is the actual useful test. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: PERICARDIAL EFFUSION/TAMPONADE: "Measurements of tumor markers in the pericardial fluid are not helpful in the diagnosis of malignant pericardial fluid."

items.assertion: Tocilizumab is used to treat severe cytokine release syndrome.
items.reason: Tocilizumab prevents IL-6 from binding to its receptors, inhibiting IL-6 signaling that drives CRS.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because IL-6 is central to CRS, blocking IL-6 receptor signaling with tocilizumab attenuates the syndrome.
items.reference: HUMAN ANTIBODY INFUSION REACTIONS: "Tocilizumab prevents IL-6 binding to both cell-associated and soluble IL-6 receptors and therefore inhibits both classical and trans-IL-6 signaling."
